items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: Why is the 4s orbital filled before the 3d orbital?
items.correct: Because the 4s orbital has a lower energy than 3d when both are empty, so electrons fill it first.
items.misconception: Because the 4s shell is closer to the nucleus than the 3d shell.
items.random_wrong: Because d orbitals can only fill after p orbitals in the next shell.
items.why: Students assume shell number directly correlates with distance/energy ordering, so 3-anything must come before 4-anything — but energy ordering crosses over at 4s/3d.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.
It's ΔG < 0. “ΔH > 0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An endothermic reaction can be spontaneous if:
items.correct: ΔS is positive and TΔS > ΔH
items.why: Correct answer: ΔS is positive and TΔS > ΔH. “ΔS is negative” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
